summary: Gruber highlights Josh Marshall's detailed account of Google's monopolistic control over the web advertising industry through DoubleClick, then pivots to his own experience of being unceremoniously dumped from Amazon's affiliate program over a decade-old article. He notes that while he's avoided the mainstream ad industry at some financial cost, he doesn't regret it. The Amazon termination—with no warning, no appeal process, and no human contact—illustrates the fundamental power imbalance between platforms and publishers. Gruber argues these tech giants aren't malicious, but their indifference to the publishers who depend on them is its own kind of problem.
key_points: Google's control of DoubleClick gives it monopoly power at every level of the ad industry, with first-look privileges and exclusive data advantages
Gruber's independent ad model sacrificed revenue but preserved autonomy from platform dependence
Amazon terminated his affiliate account over a 13-year-old article with no warning, no appeal, and no human contact
Publishers who rely on affiliate revenue are in a fundamentally asymmetric relationship where the platform can pull the plug at any moment
The problem with tech giants isn't malice but indifference—they simply don't care about the publishers on their platforms
money_quotes: I've almost certainly left a lot of money on the table over the last decade by eschewing the mainstream web advertising industry, but I don't regret it.
There was no warning. The first and only email I received about this was informing me that my account was already terminated.
But in no way is it a relationship between peers. Amazon holds all the power, and as evidenced above, they can just pull the plug at any moment, with no warning and no recourse.
They're not evil. They just don't care.
I guess that's how you say 'Fuck you' in Seattle?
It's good to be the king. It's good to be a Google.
key_insight: Publishers who build their businesses on giant platforms are serfs, not partners—the platforms aren't hostile, they're simply indifferent to the livelihoods that depend on them.
